narrative:
I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 77% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. Your profile shows strong commitments like planning to attend full-time and coming from an upper-middle-income family, but it also highlights some challenging academic markers: you ranked in the 2nd decile for Year 1 grades and the 1st decile for Year 3 grades at your school, earned a 2.7 undergraduate GPA, and scored a 20 on the LSAT— all notably below typical applicants' averages of around 5.7 and 5.5 for those deciles, 3.2 for GPA, and 36.7 for LSAT. As a female Asian student, your demographics align with smaller but present groups in the data, yet they didn't offset the academic concerns.

The model's decision was driven most heavily by your Year 3 decile ranking, which had the strongest influence in pushing the prediction toward failure compared to what it sees in successful students. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile, followed closely by your LSAT score, each contributing significantly to the high-risk assessment by signaling lower preparation readiness than the averages. Your family income level came next in influence, then your racial background, undergraduate GPA, full-time status, and gender— in that order of impact— all nudging the prediction further toward expecting a bar struggle rather than success.

In essence, while no single factor dooms you, the low rankings in later-year school performance stood out as the biggest red flags, far more than your LSAT or GPA alone, when stacked against students who typically pass. This isn't about your potential— many overcome similar starts— but about how these metrics historically correlate with bar outcomes in our data. To boost your future chances, focus on what you can realistically improve: retake the LSAT to aim closer to 35+, build stronger academic habits to lift any future grade deciles, and seek GPA-boosting opportunities like additional undergrad courses or targeted prep programs. With dedicated bar exam tutoring and study strategies tailored to your strengths, students like you have turned predictions around before. You're capable of rewriting this story— consider reapplying after addressing these core academic areas.
